claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 7 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 12 years old and lives with you.
  - Charlie is 10 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 7 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 12 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 10 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 4 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

Let me start with a few basic questions:

1. **Do you have any children?** If yes, how many children are you asking about, and what are their ages?

This will help me understand your situation and guide you through the eligibility assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have three children. Alex is 7, Blake is 12, and Charlie is 10. I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! You have three children - Alex (7), Blake (12), and Charlie (10). Let me ask you some questions to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for each of them.

Let me start with some general questions:

1. **Are you responsible for these children?** This means do they live with you, or do you contribute at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards their upkeep (even if they don't live with you full-time)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, all three of them live with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. Now let me ask:

2. **Are any of Alex, Blake, or Charlie currently in full-time non-advanced education or approved training?** 

(This is typically relevant for children aged 16-19, but I want to make sure I have complete information about their circumstances.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, they're not. They're all still in primary or secondary school - just the regular schooling for their ages.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, that's helpful. Let me continue with a few more questions:

3. **Are you or your partner currently getting Child Benefit for any of these children already?** In other words, is someone else already claiming Child Benefit for Alex, Blake, or Charlie?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody is currently claiming Child Benefit for any of them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. A few more important questions:

4. **Do any of the children (Alex, Blake, or Charlie) currently live with anyone else, or are any of them in hospital or care?** For example, are they living with another parent, relative, or in any form of local authority care?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Blake has been in local authority care for a few months now, and Charlie has been in hospital for about a month. Alex lives at home with me full-time.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that information. This is important for determining eligibility. Let me ask some follow-up questions:

5. **For Blake, who is in local authority care** - are you still contributing to Blake's upkeep financially? And do you expect Blake to return home to live with you?

6. **For Charlie, who has been in hospital for about a month** - is Charlie expected to remain in hospital for longer, or will they be coming home soon?

Let me start with Blake first - are you contributing to Blake's costs while in care, and is this a temporary arrangement?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
For Blake, no, I'm not able to contribute to their upkeep while they're in care. They're in the care home full-time and I'm not sure when or if they'll be coming back home.

For Charlie, they've been in hospital for about a month now, but I'm hoping they'll be home soon. I'm not sure exactly how much longer they'll need to stay.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. Let me ask a couple more questions to complete the assessment:

7. **Regarding Blake's care arrangement** - has it been more than 8 weeks (about 2 months) since Blake went into local authority care? You mentioned "a few months" - could you tell me approximately how long it's been?

8. **Regarding Charlie in hospital** - has it been more than 12 weeks (about 3 months) since Charlie was admitted? You mentioned about a month, so I believe it's less than 12 weeks, but I want to confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
For Blake, it's been about 12 weeks since they went into care.

For Charlie, yes, it's been about 4 weeks, so definitely less than 12 weeks.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me ask one final question:

9. **Are you currently living in the UK or otherwise eligible to claim UK benefits?** (For example, are you a UK resident or have the right to claim benefits in the UK?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't currently live in the UK. I live abroad.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Eligibility Assessment Complete

I've completed the assessment for your three children. Unfortunately, **you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for any of them**. Here's the breakdown:

### **Alex (age 7):**
❌ **Not eligible** - Alex lives with you full-time, which would normally be positive for eligibility. However, since you do not currently live in the UK, you cannot claim Child Benefit. To be eligible, you must normally live in the UK or qualify under specific circumstances (such as working abroad for a UK employer or being a Crown servant).

### **Blake (age 12):**
❌ **Not eligible** - Blake has been in local authority care for approximately 12 weeks without you contributing to their upkeep. Under UK rules, you can only continue to receive Child Benefit for a child in care for up to 8 weeks if you're contributing at least the equivalent of Child Benefit to their upkeep. Since it's been 12 weeks and you're not contributing, this exceeds the time limit. Additionally, you do not live in the UK, which is a fundamental requirement.

### **Charlie (age 10):**
❌ **Not eligible** - While Charlie has been in hospital for about 4 weeks (which is within the 12-week allowance for children in hospital), you still cannot claim because you do not currently live in the UK.

---

**The main issue is your residency status.** Child Benefit is only available to people who normally live in the UK, unless they qualify under specific exceptions. If your circumstances change and you move back to the UK, you may wish to reassess your eligibility at that time.

reasonings: Alex is 7 years old and lives with you full-time. However, you do not currently live in the UK. To be eligible for Child Benefit, you must normally live in the UK or qualify under specific circumstances (such as working abroad for a UK employer or Crown servant). Since you live abroad and have not indicated any qualifying connection to the UK, you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 12 years old and has been in local authority care for approximately 12 weeks. Under UK Child Benefit rules, you can only continue to receive Child Benefit for a child in local authority care for up to 8 weeks if you are contributing to their upkeep at a rate at least equal to Child Benefit. Since you are not contributing to Blake's upkeep and it has been 12 weeks (which exceeds the 8-week limit), you would not be eligible for Child Benefit for Blake even if you lived in the UK. Additionally, you do not currently live in the UK, which is a fundamental eligibility requirement.

Charlie is 10 years old and has been in hospital for approximately 4 weeks. Under UK Child Benefit rules, you can continue to receive Child Benefit for a child in hospital for up to 12 weeks (or 84 days). Since Charlie has been in hospital for about 4 weeks, this is within the 12-week allowance. However, you do not currently live in the UK. To be eligible for Child Benefit, you must normally live in the UK or qualify under specific circumstances. Since you live abroad and have not indicated any qualifying connection to the UK, you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Charlie.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has three children: Alex (7), Blake (12), and Charlie (10). While they are responsible for the children, the fundamental issue is that they do not currently live in the UK. Child Benefit eligibility requires that the claimant normally lives in the UK or qualifies under specific circumstances (such as being a Crown servant or working abroad for a UK-based employer). Since the claimant lives abroad without any indicated qualifying connection, they are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for any of their children. Additionally, even if UK residency were satisfied, Blake's situation (in local authority care for 12 weeks without the claimant contributing to upkeep) would exceed the 8-week limit for children in care, making that claim ineligible on separate grounds.

To be eligible for Child Benefit, you must normally live in the UK or qualify under specific circumstances. Since you live abroad and have not indicated any qualifying connection to the UK, you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Charlie.

While they are responsible for the children, the fundamental issue is that they do not currently live in the UK. Child Benefit eligibility requires that the claimant normally lives in the UK or qualifies under specific circumstances (such as being a Crown servant or working abroad for a UK-based employer). Since the claimant lives abroad without any indicated qualifying connection, they are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for any of their children. Additionally, even if UK residency were satisfied, Blake's situation (in local authority care for 12 weeks without the claimant contributing to upkeep) would exceed the 8-week limit for children in care, making that claim ineligible on separate grounds.
